teaching.title: Types of Errors in Python
teaching.analogy: Errors are like different types of alarms in a building. A SyntaxError is the fire alarm going off before you've even entered — the code can't run at all. A RuntimeError is a gas leak discovered WHILE the building is occupied — code started but crashed mid-way.
teaching.content: Python has 3 main error categories: SyntaxError (code structure is wrong — won't even start), RuntimeError/Exception (code starts but crashes during execution), and LogicError (runs fine but gives wrong answers — the sneakiest!).

examples.title: Common Python Errors
examples.explanation: Each error has a specific cause and fix.
examples.code:
# SyntaxError — Python won't even start:
if True    # missing colon!

# NameError — using undefined variable:
print(undefined_var)

# TypeError — wrong type operation:
result = '5' + 5  # can't add str + int

# IndexError — list index out of range:
arr = [1, 2, 3]
print(arr[10])   # only indices 0-2 exist!

# ZeroDivisionError:
print(10 / 0)

examples.title: Reading Error Messages
examples.explanation: Error messages tell you exactly where and what went wrong.
examples.code:
# Python error message format:
# Traceback (most recent call last):
#   File 'main.py', line 5, in <module>
#     print(x)
# NameError: name 'x' is not defined

# Read from BOTTOM UP:
# 1. Error type: NameError
# 2. Message: 'x' is not defined
# 3. Line: line 5

teaching.funFact: Python has 65+ built-in exception types! They form a hierarchy — all inherit from BaseException. The most common ones: TypeError, ValueError, KeyError, IndexError, AttributeError. 🎯

teaching.title: ValueError & TypeError Deep Dive
teaching.analogy: TypeError is a bouncer who checks your ID — if you try to get into the 'integer math' club with a string, you're turned away. ValueError is a quality inspector — the TYPE is correct, but the VALUE is unacceptable (like int('hello') — it's a string, but not a valid number).
teaching.content: TypeError happens when an operation is applied to the wrong type. ValueError happens when a function receives the right type but an inappropriate value. Both are extremely common when handling user input.

examples.title: TypeError vs ValueError
examples.explanation: Know the difference to fix bugs faster.
examples.code:
# TypeError: wrong type
result = '10' + 5      # str + int = TypeError
len(42)                # len() needs sequence, not int

# ValueError: right type, wrong value
int('hello')           # 'hello' is str (correct!) but not a number
float('abc')           # same problem
int('3.14')            # '3.14' is not a pure integer string

# Fix: validate before converting:
user = input('Enter number: ')
if user.isdigit():
    print(int(user))

examples.title: KeyError & AttributeError
examples.explanation: Two more common ones to recognize.
examples.code:
# KeyError: accessing a key that doesn't exist
data = {'name': 'Alex'}
print(data['age'])  # KeyError: 'age'

# AttributeError: method doesn't exist on this type
x = 42
x.upper()  # AttributeError: int has no .upper()

teaching.funFact: The most Googled Python error is 'IndexError: list index out of range'. Every programmer has hit it. The fix: check len(list) before accessing by index! 📊

mcq.q: Which error occurs when you try to access an undefined variable?
mcq.explanation: Error Intel: NameError means Python can't find a name (variable/function) in any scope. Usually caused by: typo in variable name, using before defining, or forgetting to import.
ValueError: The TYPE is correct (it's a string) but the VALUE is invalid — 'hello' can't be converted to an integer. int() requires a string that looks like a number: int('42') works.

mcq.q: Which error type means Python can't even START running your code?
mcq.explanation: Compile Time: SyntaxError is caught BEFORE execution. Python reads your entire file and validates structure first. A missing colon, unmatched bracket, or bad indentation causes SyntaxError.

mcq.q: arr = [1, 2, 3]. What error does arr[5] raise?
mcq.explanation: IndexError: The list has indices 0, 1, 2 only. Accessing index 5 is out of bounds. This is one of the most common errors in loops — always check len() or use range(len(arr)).

mcq.q: d = {'a': 1}. What error does d['b'] raise?
mcq.explanation: KeyError: Dictionary key 'b' doesn't exist. Fix: use d.get('b') which returns None instead of crashing, or check 'b' in d first.

coding.title: Name the Error
coding.objective: What error does '5' + 5 raise? Type the error class name.
coding.hint: You're mixing incompatible types — string + integer.

coding.title: Index Boundary
coding.objective: arr = [10, 20, 30]. What is the highest valid index?

coding.title: Safe Dict Access
coding.objective: d = {'x': 5}. What does d.get('y') return if 'y' doesn't exist?
coding.hint: .get() returns None instead of raising KeyError for missing keys.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug:

name = input('Name: ')
print('Hello ' + name + '!')
age = int(name)
print('Age doubled:', age * 2)
options: int(name) will raise ValueError if name is not a number — should use a separate input for age
debugging.answer: int(name) will raise ValueError if name is not a number — should use a separate input for age
debugging.hint: name holds a text name like 'Alex'. int('Alex') raises ValueError. Get age separately.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug:

scores = [95, 87, 72]
for i in range(4):
    print(scores[i])
options: range(4) goes 0,1,2,3 but list only has indices 0,1,2 — use range(len(scores)) or range(3)
debugging.answer: range(4) goes 0,1,2,3 but list only has indices 0,1,2 — use range(len(scores)) or range(3)
debugging.hint: range(4) includes index 3, but scores only has 3 elements (indices 0-2). IndexError on last iteration.

options: Nothing wrong, 18 is not an adult
Should be age >= 18 — 18 year olds ARE adults (off-by-one logic error)
debugging.answer: Should be age >= 18 — 18 year olds ARE adults (off-by-one logic error)
debugging.hint: Logic errors don't crash — they silently give wrong answers. Age 18 should return True for adult.

prediction.explanation: .get(key, default) returns the default value if the key isn't found. 'c' not in dict → returns 'not found'.
int('abc') raises ValueError. The except block catches it and prints 'bad'. No crash!

teaching.title: try / except — Error Handling
teaching.analogy: try/except is like a safety net under a tightrope walker. You TRY to walk the rope (run the risky code). If you fall (error occurs), the EXCEPT net catches you. The show goes on — no catastrophic crash!
teaching.content: Wrap risky code in a try block. If an error occurs, Python jumps to the matching except block instead of crashing. You can catch specific error types or use a general except.

examples.title: Basic try/except
examples.explanation: The except block runs only when an error occurs in try.

examples.title: Catching Exception Details
examples.explanation: 'as e' gives you the error message.

teaching.funFact: Using bare 'except:' (catching ALL errors) is bad practice — it hides real bugs! Always catch specific exception types so unexpected errors still surface. 🎯

teaching.title: else, finally & raise
teaching.analogy: 'finally' is like the cleanup crew — they come in whether the show was a success or a disaster. 'else' is the backstage celebration that only happens if the show went perfectly. 'raise' is the director choosing to deliberately trigger the alarm.
teaching.content: try/except can have else (runs if NO error occurred) and finally (ALWAYS runs — perfect for cleanup). raise lets you throw your own exceptions intentionally.

examples.title: else & finally
examples.explanation: else = success path. finally = always runs.
examples.code:
try:
    result = int('42')
except ValueError:
    print('Bad input')
else:
    print(f'Success! Got {result}')  # only if no error
finally:
    print('Always runs!')            # cleanup code here

# Output:
# Success! Got 42
# Always runs!

examples.title: raise your own exceptions
examples.explanation: You can throw exceptions deliberately to enforce rules.

teaching.funFact: finally is used everywhere in real code — especially to close files, release database connections, or unlock resources. Even if an exception crashes the try block, finally runs! 🔒

options: Runs if an error occurs in the try block
mcq.explanation: Exception Handling: The except block is the safety net — it only executes when an error/exception occurs inside the try block. If try completes without error, except is skipped.

options: Always — whether error occurred or not
mcq.explanation: Finally Always: The finally block executes REGARDLESS of whether try succeeded or failed. It's used for guaranteed cleanup: closing files, releasing connections, freeing resources.

mcq.q: What does 'except ValueError as e' give you?
options: The variable e holds the error message/details
mcq.explanation: Error Details: 'as e' binds the exception object to variable e. You can then print(e) or str(e) to get the error description. Essential for debugging and user-friendly error messages.

mcq.q: Which block runs ONLY if the try block had NO errors?
mcq.explanation: Success Path: The else block after try/except runs ONLY when no exception occurred. It's the 'happy path' code that doesn't belong in try (to avoid accidentally catching its errors).
raise: You can manually trigger any exception with raise. This enforces rules in your code — if someone calls set_age(-5), you raise ValueError to signal invalid input.

coding.title: Try Block
coding.objective: What keyword starts the block of risky code?
coding.hint: The 3-letter keyword that marks risky code.

coding.title: Catch Specific Error
coding.objective: Which error should you catch for int('hello') failing?
coding.hint: int() raises this when given a non-numeric string.

coding.title: Finally Block
coding.objective: What keyword marks the block that ALWAYS runs?
coding.hint: It always runs — success or failure.

options: Bare 'except:' catches ALL errors including unexpected ones, hiding real bugs. Use 'except ValueError:' specifically
debugging.answer: Bare 'except:' catches ALL errors including unexpected ones, hiding real bugs. Use 'except ValueError:' specifically
debugging.hint: Always catch specific exception types. Bare except hides bugs silently!

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug — wrong order:

try:
    x = 10 / 0
finally:
    print('done')
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print('zero!')
options: finally must come AFTER except blocks, not before them
debugging.answer: finally must come AFTER except blocks, not before them
debugging.hint: Order: try → except → else → finally. finally always goes last.

prediction.explanation: 10/2=5, no error. except skipped. else runs ('success'). finally ALWAYS runs ('done'). Output: success↵done.
check(-3) raises ValueError('negative!'). The except catches it and prints 'Caught: negative!'.

teaching.title: Opening & Reading Files
teaching.analogy: Working with files in Python is like using a physical filing cabinet. You open the drawer (open()), read the document (read()), and close the drawer when done (close()). The 'with' statement is a smart handle — it closes the drawer automatically even if you trip and fall!
teaching.content: open(filename, mode) creates a connection to a file. Mode 'r' = read (default), 'w' = write (creates/overwrites), 'a' = append. Always use 'with open()' so the file closes automatically.

examples.title: Reading a File
examples.explanation: with open() is the safest pattern.
examples.code:
# Read entire file as one string:
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()
    print(content)

# Read line by line:
with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())  # strip() removes \n

examples.title: read() vs readlines()
examples.explanation: Different ways to read file content.
examples.code:
with open('scores.txt', 'r') as f:
    # read() = one big string
    text = f.read()
    
    # readline() = next one line
    line = f.readline()
    
    # readlines() = list of all lines
    lines = f.readlines()

teaching.funFact: The 'with' statement uses a Python concept called Context Managers. They guarantee cleanup (file closing) even if an exception crashes mid-read. Without 'with', a crash could lock the file! 🔒

teaching.title: Writing & Appending Files
teaching.analogy: Mode 'w' is like replacing a whiteboard — you erase everything and start fresh. Mode 'a' is like adding a sticky note to the bottom — you keep everything already there and just add to the end.
teaching.content: Mode 'w' creates the file if it doesn't exist and overwrites if it does. Mode 'a' adds to the end without deleting existing content. write() writes a string; writelines() writes a list of strings.

examples.title: Writing to a File
examples.code:
# Write mode — creates new or overwrites:
with open('log.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('First line\n')
    f.write('Second line\n')

# Append mode — adds to end:
with open('log.txt', 'a') as f:
    f.write('Third line\n')

examples.title: Writing Lists
examples.explanation: writelines() writes each item — add \n manually.
examples.code:
scores = ['100\n', '95\n', '87\n']
with open('scores.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.writelines(scores)

# Or use a loop:
with open('output.txt', 'w') as f:
    for item in scores:
        f.write(item)

teaching.funFact: Python can also read/write JSON files using the json module: json.dump() writes, json.load() reads. This is how most web apps save configuration and data! 📊

mcq.q: Which file mode creates a new file or overwrites an existing one?
mcq.explanation: Write Mode: 'w' opens file for writing — creates it if it doesn't exist, or completely overwrites it if it does. Dangerous with important files! Use 'a' to preserve existing content.

mcq.q: Why should you use 'with open()' instead of just open()?
options: It automatically closes the file even if an error occurs
It's the only syntax that works
mcq.explanation: Context Manager: 'with open() as f' uses Python's context manager protocol. It GUARANTEES the file is closed when the with-block exits — even if an exception occurs inside. Eliminates resource leaks.

options: A list of strings, one per line
mcq.explanation: readlines(): Returns a LIST of strings, one string per line. Each string includes the \n newline character. Use .strip() on each line to remove it.

mcq.q: Which mode adds content to the END of an existing file?
mcq.explanation: Append Mode: 'a' (append) opens the file and positions the cursor at the END. New content is added after existing content — nothing is deleted. Perfect for log files.

coding.title: Read Mode
coding.objective: What mode string opens a file for reading?
coding.starterCode:
with open('data.txt', '___') as f:
    content = f.read()

coding.title: Write Mode
coding.objective: What mode string creates/overwrites a file for writing?
coding.hint: w = write. Warning: overwrites existing content!

coding.title: Append Mode
coding.objective: What mode adds to the end without deleting existing content?
coding.starterCode:
with open('log.txt', '___') as f:
    f.write('New entry\n')
coding.hint: a = append. Adds to the end.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug — file not closed:

f = open('data.txt', 'r')
content = f.read()
print(content)
# f.close() — forgot to close!
options: Should use 'with open()' to guarantee automatic closing: with open('data.txt') as f:
debugging.answer: Should use 'with open()' to guarantee automatic closing: with open('data.txt') as f:
debugging.hint: Always use 'with open()' — it closes the file automatically even if an error occurs.

debugging.description:
🚨 Bug — overwrites instead of appends:

with open('log.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('New entry\n')
# All previous log entries are gone!
options: Mode 'w' overwrites the file. Use 'a' to append to existing log entries
debugging.answer: Mode 'w' overwrites the file. Use 'a' to append to existing log entries
debugging.hint: 'w' = write (overwrites everything). 'a' = append (adds to end, keeps existing).

prediction.code:
with open('test.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Line 1\n')
    f.write('Line 2\n')

with open('test.txt', 'r') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()
    print(len(lines))
prediction.explanation: We wrote 2 lines. readlines() returns a list of lines: ['Line 1\n', 'Line 2\n']. len() = 2.

prediction.code:
with open('num.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('42')

with open('num.txt', 'r') as f:
    data = f.read()
    print(type(data))
prediction.explanation: f.read() always returns a STRING — even if the file contains a number. You must convert: int(f.read()) to get an integer.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE HANDLER
teaching.analogy: The Handler controls all the ways your program can break. It knows every error type, every exception trap, and every file operation pitfall. Prove your code is bulletproof!
teaching.content: THE HANDLER tests everything from Level 6: error types (TypeError, ValueError, KeyError, IndexError), try/except/else/finally, raise, file modes (r/w/a), with open(), read/write operations.

examples.title: What you've mastered
examples.code:
✅ TypeError, ValueError, KeyError, IndexError
✅ try / except / else / finally
✅ raise — custom exceptions
✅ 'as e' to capture error details
✅ open() modes: r, w, a
✅ with open() context manager
✅ read(), readlines(), write()

teaching.funFact: Production software at companies like Google and Amazon has error handling in EVERY external call — network requests, file operations, database queries. Unhandled exceptions in production can cost millions! 💰

mcq.q:
What is the output?
try:
    print(1/0)
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print('zero')
finally:
    print('end')
mcq.explanation: 1/0 raises ZeroDivisionError → except prints 'zero'. finally ALWAYS runs → prints 'end'. Output: zero then end.

mcq.q: Which error does this raise: d = {}; d['key']?
mcq.explanation: KeyError: Accessing a dictionary key that doesn't exist raises KeyError. Fix with d.get('key') or check 'key' in d first.

mcq.q: You want to add to an existing log file without deleting it. Which mode?
mcq.explanation: Append Mode: 'a' opens the file and positions the cursor at the END. New writes are added AFTER existing content. 'w' would destroy the existing log!

options: Deliberately triggers a ValueError with message 'bad input'
mcq.explanation: raise: Manually triggers an exception. Used to enforce business rules: if age < 0: raise ValueError('Age must be positive'). Can be caught with except ValueError.

mcq.q: f.read() on a file containing '123' returns what type?
mcq.explanation: Always String: f.read() ALWAYS returns a string — regardless of file content. Numbers in files are strings until you explicitly convert: int(f.read()) or float(f.read()).

coding.title: Boss: Safe Number Input
coding.objective: Which error type should you catch when doing int(input())?
coding.hint: int() raises this when the string can't be converted to integer.

coding.title: Boss: File Read Mode
coding.objective: Complete: open the file for reading.
coding.starterCode:
with open('data.txt', '___') as f:
    text = f.read()

debugging.description:
🚨 BOSS BUG:

try:
    with open('data.txt', 'r') as f:
        content = f.read()
except:
    pass

print(content)
options: If the file doesn't exist, the except catches the error but content is never defined — print(content) raises NameError
debugging.answer: If the file doesn't exist, the except catches the error but content is never defined — print(content) raises NameError
debugging.hint: Define content = '' before the try block so it always exists, even if the file read fails.

prediction.explanation: int('5') succeeds — no ValueError. except skipped. else runs ('good'). finally always runs ('always').
